data.title: Tesla: Bearish Reaction From 200-Day MA With Weak Relative Strength (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Kelvin Wong

Tesla (TSLA), the first US mega-cap stock (the Magnificent 7 group), will report its Q1 2026 earnings results after the close of today’s (Wednesday, 22 Apr 2026) US trading session.

The consensus forecast anticipates a slight increase in earnings growth, from $ 0.27 to $0.35 earnings per share (EPS) in Q1, representing a 30% rise over the same quarter a year ago.

Tesla has lagged the market and is the worst performer among the Magnificent 7

Since the ceasefire of the US-Iran war on 8 April 2026, the bullish animal spirits have reemerged in the US stock market, as the Magnificent 7 group of mega-cap stocks rallied, taking reference from the pre-war baseline of 27 February 2026 to Tuesday, 21 April 2026, led by Amazon (+19%), Nvidia (+12.8%), and Microsoft (+8%).

In contrast, Tesla underperformed, with a loss of 4%, and it also underperformed year-to-date (as of April 21, 2026), with a double-digit loss of 14.1% (see Fig. 1 & Fig. 2).

Medium-term technical outlook of Tesla (1 to 3 weeks)

Bearish bias below 417.40 key medium-term pivotal resistance. A break below 363.80 intermediate support opens the scope for further potential weakness to expose the medium-term supports at 337.25/328.20 (also the 61.8% Fibonacci retracement of prior up move from 7 April 2025 low to 22 December 2025 high) and 300.05/288.80 (also the 76.4% Fibonacci retracement of prior up move from 7 April 2025 low to 22 December 2025 high) (see Fig. 3).

On the flip side, a clearance with a daily close above 417.40 invalidates the bearish scenario for a further recovery towards 437.40/450.20, followed by a potential retest at its current all-time high of 498.83.

Key elements to support the medium-term bearish bias on Tesla

  • The price actions of TSLA since 18 December 2024 high of 488.54 has started to form an impending major “Double Top” bearish reversal configuration.
  • The recent 21% rebound from its 7 April 2026 low has stalled and shaped a bearish reaction right after a retest on the key 200-day moving average.
  • The daily volatility-adjusted relative strength (VARS) of Tesla against the S&P 500 exchange-traded fund has trended lower below its zero line since 26 January 2026, which suggests the ongoing medium-term underperformance of TSLA against the SPY remains intact.
  • The daily RSI momentum indicator of TSLA has just staged a bearish reaction from its descending resistance at the 60 level, which suggests the lack of upside momentum.

Original Post

data.title: Schrodinger: AI Threat Is Looming Large (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Growing concerns around AI have weighed on Schrödinger's (SDGR) share price in recent months, a situation that has been exacerbated by relatively weak software growth and guidance for 2026. Doubts seem to be springing largely from the progress that Isomorphic Labs is making with its AI models.

I previously suggested that the decline in Schrödinger's stock was being driven by temporary market headwinds. At the time I felt the company's valuation was compelling, though, despite the potential for further downside in the near term. The stock is down around 25% since then.

While Schrödinger's software is unlikely to be displaced in the short term, there appears to be a realistic scenario where its use is limited to certain niches. The downstream value that the company is in line to receive probably more than justifies its current market capitalization, but it is difficult to see investor perception of the business changing anytime soon.

Market Conditions

Schrödinger continues to face a challenging demand environment (elevated interest rates, tight biopharma budgets, academic funding pressure), but recent share price weakness is probably being driven by concerns around long-term competitive positioning. This primarily relates to Isomorphic Labs recent Drug Design Engine (IsoDDE) announcement. IsoDDE builds on AlphaFold 3, improving accuracy and generalizability. It also predicts small molecule binding affinities with accuracies that exceed physics-based methods while being faster and lower cost.

Schrödinger Business Updates

While the tangible benefits of Schrödinger's software have already been demonstrated (accelerated development timelines and improved probability of success), there is a growing question around the long-term impact of AI. AI is also a force multiplier for Schrödinger's software, though, enabling companies to explore more chemical space. Despite this, there is a growing risk of displacement long-term.

Schrödinger believes that the data AI models are trained on is a significant limitation. Protein structure output from experimental methods and AI can be low-resolution, with Schrödinger suggesting that the structure of only around 10-15% of the human proteome is known with high resolution. The ability to accurately predict novel structures has also been limited so far. While Schrödinger is working on methods to improve the resolution of structures produced using AI, IsoDDE suggests that many of the limitations of AI are addressable.

Concerns about displacement by AI have likely been exacerbated by the recent weak financial performance of Schrödinger's software business. In response, Schrödinger is now emphasizing expansion opportunities, including materials science. Materials science revenue totaled 17 million USD in 2025, up from 15 million USD a year earlier. Applications include consumer packaged goods, chemicals, and electronics, with battery chemistry a particular focus area.

Within drug discovery, Schrödinger is working on areas like toxicology, synthetic chemistry, drug formulations, and improved support for biologics. Newly released products include:

  • Predictive toxicology for drug safety
  • Support for biologics
  • Crystal structure prediction that supports formulation
  • Retrosynthetic analysis
  • Agentic AI capabilities

Schrödinger's toxicology solution is now out of beta, and feedback has been strong. Toxicology remains an important challenge in drug discovery and often results in significant time and resources being wasted, meaning this solution could be a growth driver going forward. There are something like 20,000 proteins for which avoiding binding is desirable, though, and Schrödinger's software can only handle around 60-70 proteins at the moment.

Within the Drug Discovery business, Schrödinger plans on completing the Phase I studies of SGR-1505 and SGR-3515, with SGR-3515 data expected in the second quarter. Schrödinger has deemphasized its internal programs as collaboration demand has rebounded, though, and these programs are expected to be advanced through partners.

Schrödinger is also now emphasizing its "Modality Switch" programs, which have the same biological target but a different therapeutic modality, reducing biological risk. Schrödinger has already successfully discovered oral versions of injectable antibodies and peptides. Oral versions of antibodies and peptides are a large opportunity (tens of hundreds of billions of dollars) and can improve accessibility and costs. For example, cardiometabolic drugs like Mounjaro and Wegovy and inflammatory and immunology drugs like Humira.

On the partnership front, there are 20 separate collaborators and 16 programs for which Schrödinger is eligible for royalties on sales, 5 of which are targeting 5+ billion USD markets. Pharma royalties are generally in the mid-single- to low-double-digit range. Schrödinger is also eligible for up to 5 billion USD in milestones and owns a small piece of several successful biopharma companies. Schrödinger has already generated value from its equity stakes in Nimbus, Relay, Morphic, and Petra and retains equity positions in Nimbus, Ajax, and Structure Therapeutics.

Financial Analysis

While software revenue increased 11% in 2025, it was down 13% YoY in the fourth quarter, and ACV was fairly flat. Q4 2025 was weaker in terms of multi-year on-prem deals, which have an outsized impact on revenue. Software growth is being driven by hosted and maintenance revenues, although the transition towards hosted software is a headwind in terms of revenue recognition.

Total ACV grew 4% in 2025 to 198.5 million USD, with larger customers driving growth. Gates Foundation, Government/Academic customers, and smaller life sciences customers weighed on ACV growth in 2025, though. Schrödinger is focused on growing adoption within customers so that they are spending more than 1 million USD annually. Net dollar retention fell to 100%, after several years averaging above 110%, which Schrödinger believes is largely related to macro conditions. Gross dollar retention is 96%, though, indicating that churn is modest. It is worth noting that 2 of Schrödinger's largest customers were acquired in 2025, and while this is not expected to impact spend, it is a risk.

Schrödinger expects 218-228 million USD ACV in 2026, an increase of 10-15% YoY, and is targeting 10-15% annual software ACV growth over the next few years. Drug Discovery revenue is expected to be 55-65 million USD in 2026, and Schrödinger doesn't appear to be expecting material growth from its Drug Discovery business going forward.

Schrödinger is transitioning customers from on-prem to hosted deployments, which is expected to be a material revenue headwind over the next few years. Roughly a quarter of Schrödinger's software revenue is from hosted deployments at the moment, and the company is targeting 75% by 2028. Each 1% increase in hosted revenue contribution is expected to reduce revenue by 2-3 million USD in the current year. The impact of this will be more pronounced in 2027 and 2028, with revenue expected to begin converging with ACV by 2028.

Schrödinger's software gross margin declined to 74% in 2025. Drug Discovery margins have rebounded somewhat with scale, but the mix shift towards Drug Discovery has been a headwind. The transition to hosted revenue is also weighing on gross margins at the moment.

Schrödinger's operating expenses totaled 310 million USD in 2025, a decline of 9% YoY. This was driven by cost-cutting efforts and the transition of R&D expenses to COGS as the Drug Discovery business grows. Sales and marketing expenses were up modestly, though, with Schrödinger investing in long-term software growth. Schrödinger also reported a 65 million USD other income gain, which was largely related to mark-to-market changes in its equity investments.

Operating expenses are expected to decline again in 2026. Despite this, Schrödinger isn't expecting to achieve adjusted EBITDA profitability until 2028. This situation isn't as bad as it seems, though, as Schrödinger has so far avoided significantly diluting investors through downstream value creation.

Conclusion

Schrödinger's stock has been crushed by AI concerns in recent months, a situation that has been exacerbated by relatively weak software growth. While AI is a real risk, I still believe that Schrödinger is deeply undervalued. Even if the worst AI fears come true, displacement is likely to occur fairly slowly

The company only has a little over 900 million USD market capitalization, which is modest considering it generated over 250 million USD revenue in 2025 and has over 400 million USD cash. In addition, Schrödinger still stands to receive significant value from milestones and royalties.

Even though I believe that Schrödinger is likely to reward shareholders at some point, it is unclear what the catalyst will be for a turnaround at this point. This means that patience is required, and in the meantime, AI risk is likely to grow.

data.title: U.K. Inflation Heads Towards 4%, But Rate Hikes Off The Table For Now
data.description:
By James Smith, Developed Markets Economist, UK

The latest rise in UK headline CPI tells us virtually nothing about the scale and duration of the inflation wave to come. The Bank of England is still flying blind, with the conflict unresolved. But the limited amount of survey data available so far suggests little cause for alarm on inflation. And against a fragile jobs market, we don’t expect a rate hike next week or this year.

As for that March data, there was nothing too surprising. The combination of higher motor fuel and heating oil prices added roughly 40bp to headline inflation, which now sits at 3.3%. Services inflation was up, though only because of volatile airfares; our estimation of the Bank of England’s preferred gauge of “core services” stayed at 4.2%.

On ING’s base case for oil and natural gas prices – which has the former staying between 90 and 100 USD/bbl in the second and third quarters of the year, and the latter averaging 55 EUR/MWh this quarter – we’d expect UK inflation to peak fractionally above 4% in August/September but generally bouncing around a 3.5-4% range in the second half of the year. That’s consistent with a 25% rise in household electricity/gas bills when the Ofgem price cap is next updated in July. And these forecasts assume some uptick in food inflation later this year.

Wholesale natural gas prices close to pre-war levels

Since we last updated our scenarios, natural gas prices have come under renewed downward pressure. And remarkably, futures prices for delivery 12 months into the future are within spitting distance of their pre-war levels. And this matters for the Ofgem price cap, which tries to mimic what energy providers pay to lock in future supplies. If wholesale prices stay where they are today, the July price cap is more likely to rise by a mere 10-15% and retrace much of that increase in October. That’s consistent with inflation peaking around 3.5%.

So long as inflation doesn’t spike materially above 4% – a level above which the Bank has identified as being more likely to trigger a persistent bout of price pressure – we think the BoE will prefer to keep rates on hold this year.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Red Cat: A High-Risk Bet On The Future Of Warfare
data.description:
Red Cat Holdings, Inc. (RCAT) is seeing tremendous growth. The company is targeting a fast-growing drone market that continues to see an increase in demand. The growth and market in which Red Cat operates is what drew me to the stock in the first place and why I gave it a buy rating when I previously reviewed the stock. The stock has performed well since I last reviewed it to start the year. A lot changes in a few months with a company moving as fast as Red Cat. Furthermore, world events are keeping investors on their toes, particularly with defense stocks.

Recent global conflicts are increasing demand and the need for their technology and products. The company saw revenues increase to a point of scale in the fourth quarter. The company has grown its manufacturing capabilities in an impressive way. It has come at a cost to margins, but it has positioned itself to capitalize on future growth.

Margins and expenses continue to be a concern for the company. It will be something to continue to watch as the company starts to produce at scale. At current margins and expense rates, the company will take years to get itself to a point of profitability.

There are risks, as the company is burning cash and is a long way from profitability. I think overall, the market trends and strength outweigh the risks. The demand is growing, and the company has positioned itself to take advantage of that growth.

Financials

The company reported a large step up in revenue and volume during Q4. Revenue in Q4 was $26.2 million; this is up from $1.3 million in the prior year. The more impressive part was the growth sequentially from Q3, which was 172%. For the full year, the company reported revenue of $40.7 million, an increase of 161% from the prior year. Q4 was really the quarter where the company scaled production, and revenues showed the result.

While the revenue growth was fantastic, the gross margin continued to be a major concern. The company reported a gross margin of 4.2% for the quarter. This is really bad when you consider that management guided for a gross margin of around 10% for the quarter. This is also down sequentially, even though the company did a much higher volume. I attribute this to the costs of getting the business to the scale it is now operating at. The company has built out its capacity in a drastic way over the past year. The gross margin has suffered during that time, but I expect the company to begin to work at scale and incorporate efficiencies into the process. I am not as worried about the gross margin right now due to the stage the company is at, but it will quickly become a concern if improvement is not seen over the second half of 2026. A low gross margin is going to make it difficult to ever turn a profit. The company had previously guided for a gross margin in the low 20s for 2026. I am not that optimistic after Q1. I think it will be much more likely closer to 10%-15% for the year.

The other concern is related to high operating expenses. Those operating expenses continued to increase at a rapid rate as well. Operating expenses increased to $25 million during the fourth quarter, up from $18.2 million in Q3. Most of that is related to G&A expenses as well, with it reaching $16.5 million. I understand that the company is in rapid growth mode, but those expenses continue to expand at a fast rate, especially the G&A expenses. Although it is a much slower pace than revenue. One thing that is trending down at a rapid rate is the operating expense as a percentage of revenue. This is the first quarter where revenues exceeded operating expenses. In Q3, operating expenses were double revenues. So the company is trending in the right direction and quickly. It has a long way to go, though. The operating expenses plus low gross margin are going to make it tough to become profitable.

In my previous article, I did a high-level estimate on how long it might take for the company to be profitable and also how much revenue it would require based on gross margin and current operating expenses. The profitability point got pushed out even further based on the current quarterly results. Based upon a 30% gross margin, the company's long-term goal, and annualizing the current operating expenses, the company would have to generate revenue of approximately $335 million to break even. The company is seeing strong revenue growth, but that revenue figure is a long way out. The company either needs to improve the gross margin or reduce its operating expenses as it goes forward.

The company has done multiple capital raises during the year. This has diluted shareholders, but it has put the company in a good cash position. The company ended the quarter with $167.9 million in cash. This gives the company flexibility and time to build its operations and get to a point of positive cash flow. The company also has options with M&A with the cash position.

Blue Ops

The Blue Ops business has developed at a rapid rate. This division was launched last year. The company really dug in and got to work getting a product designed and developed. They started demonstrating their USV at the end of last year. That is quick development and completion from the company. The company then got a lease on a 155,000-square-foot factory and converted it to manufacture their boats. Management noted during the earnings call that they expected to be at full-rate production by the end of March. They also noted, “We believe and are confident that they can build over 100-plus USVs in 2026 as we ramp up production capability to thousands.” The company expects to deliver the first USV boat in Q2. To go from the idea to a year later being at full-rate production in a year's time is very impressive.

The company held its innovation day at the facility. It also demonstrated some of their USV technology at the event. It really shows me the capabilities that have been built into the unit in the past year. This operational unit was a bit of a wildcard to me when I previously reviewed the stock, but it is developing much faster than I had anticipated. It looks like it should begin to add to revenues in 2026 at a much higher rate than I expected.

Drones

The company has had some positive and not-so-positive news come out of the drone business over the past few months. The company is starting to see more adoption of its drones around the world, particularly in Ukraine. The company also received some bad news about missing the cut for the Drone Dominance Program.

We can start with the bad news. I discussed at length the Drone Dominance Program in my last article. I will keep it brief here. This is, in essence, a program for the Department of Defense to source domestically manufactured drones. It is a competition where drone manufacturers are invited to have their drones tested by the military. The first stage had 11 companies chosen and receive orders for drones. Red Cat was not selected as one of the companies for Gauntlet I of the program. This is a big hit for the company. We are talking about tens of millions in steady revenues. This would have helped the company continue building out its drone business.

The company can and still plans to compete in Gauntlet II of the program. Just because the company was not selected in Gauntlet I does not mean it will not be selected in Gauntlet II. It is also important to note that just because it was not selected as part of the Drone Dominance Program does not mean that the company will not receive orders through the military. Winning in that program would have been a boost for the business for sure. It would have locked in a large amount of orders. But it does not mean that it is not going to win other contracts and programs outside the Drone Dominance Program. I do think it will be more difficult in Gauntlet II by missing out on Gauntlet I. This could turn out to be a longer-term miss for the company.

The company deployed a team to Ukraine with their Black Widow drone. The team was looking to get Black Widow into the field and make sure it is capable of doing what it is supposed to do. They are hoping to replace some Chinese-made drones that are being utilized in the country. The company's COO discussed more about this on the call, as he had returned from Ukraine the day before. It was noted that their products were being used in battle and were performing well. In fact, the company has received a letter of request from Ukrainian forces to provide the Black Widow drone. The company has established an office in Ukraine to meet the constant needs of the market there.

Manufacturing Capacity

Manufacturing capabilities were a big topic of discussion. The company really ramped up production capacity, which was seen during Q4. The company has grown its facility capacity from 36,000 square feet two years ago to over 254,000 square feet today. The other key part of this is that the company has an NDAA-compliant supply chain. This allows it to compete on many contracts that others cannot. Their USA manufacturing and sourcing have started to help set the company apart from certain competitors.

The company has expanded its Black Widow drone facility in Utah. They hit a capacity of 50 drones manufactured a day. The company expects to be at a manufacturing capacity of 1,000 Black Widow drones a month in the first half of the year. It was noted that this capacity is done on one shift. The company estimates it can triple the number of manufacturing lines and also add more shifts.

The Flightwave facility has the capability to manufacture 130 drones per month. That is only using ⅓ of the available space as well.

I discussed Blue Ops already, but the company noted that they have room to expand their capacity at their current facility to 100 boats a month.

The key with all these manufacturing facilities is that the company has ramped up capabilities significantly in a short time and positioned itself for growth. The growth will not come at as high an expense either. They do not have to build new facilities; they already have the facilities and only have to expand production. This massive buildout is also a contributor to a lower gross margin. I fully expect to start to see the gross margin increase over the second half of the year. Now that the buildout is there, it can begin to work on scale and efficiency.

Outlook

The company declined to provide annual guidance during the quarterly announcement and ensuing call. The company got burned pretty good last year on the guidance provided. I think there was a bit of a learning curve with government contracts and the amount of time it takes to get those deals done. I know this was a concern for many investors, but it does not bother me too much. The company is new in the industry, and it takes some time to learn the government sales pipeline and convert that into dollars. I don't think there is any value in providing inaccurate estimates. There is no indication that the lack of guidance was due to a slowing business. Management noted they expect continued growth during the year.

During the Q&A, there were questions around revenue estimates. Management noted the following: “There's a range from all of you out there between $100 million and $170 million…. We're comfortable in the top half of that, but we're not ready to commit to it until we get contracts in hand to give official guidance.” Based upon this commentary, analysts adjusted their estimate a bit higher than they were previously and now have the company at $148.8 million for 2026.

The other item that came out of the call was the actual volume of drones being used in Ukraine. I have been covering multiple drone companies, and there is not a lot of information provided on volumes and market potential. Everyone knows there is demand, but how much? The COO stated that Ukraine told them they are going through 350,000 ISR drones per year. That is a massive number and market potential. Red Cat is producing 1,000 drones a month. The company cannot even produce a tiny fraction of the number of drones being used in Ukraine per year. It does not even make a dent in the demand. This shows the demand and size of the market potential within the drone industry.

The company also still has its SRR program, which they expect to enter full-rate production soon. Management also noted that they expect orders to come out of Operation Epic Fury for the drone business. Management also noted they are currently building 1,000 drones a month. This is due to the demand they are already seeing in the market.

The Strait of Hormuz and issues currently happening there have brought a lot of added attention to the Blue Ops division of the business. Management noted that they are seeing increased demand from Gulf States as well as the Navy. Managing and controlling the Strait of Hormuz has become a critical talking point. Red Cat's technology provides the option of using USVs to patrol the Strait to provide counterprotection from the shore of Iran. I expect the company will generate more revenues from the Blue Ops division than I anticipated, and it could easily push the company to beat annual estimates for the year. The company previously noted that they expect revenue of $750k to $1.5 million for each vessel. The company was going to use its first 15 vessels for demonstrations, but due to demand, they now plan to ship those to customers. The company could end the year with 25+ sales, providing a boost to revenues in 2026.

Valuation

The company is valued based on future potential. Its current financials leave some wanting with the losses that I don't expect to turn positive for some time. The revenue growth and market demand are really strong enough to offset the negative profits.

The company has a market cap of $1.54 billion. The P/S ratio on the TTM is 30.9. That is a high P/S, but the company is just getting started on its growth stage and reaching scale. Revenues are expected to grow by over 250% for the year. This brings the P/S down really quickly, with a 2026 expected P/S of 10.36. I also think the revenue estimates are conservative. We have already seen analysts raise estimates since I wrote my last article and noted that revenue estimates were too low. I still think the company will beat the current revenue estimates, making the multiples lower.

The company operates at a loss, and as I noted in the calculation on earning a profit, it is likely to continue to have losses for multiple years. The valuation is rich for the company. I also think the market potential makes this rich valuation more reasonable. While it trades at high multiples and is operating at a loss, the company is really just getting started in the market and has high growth potential going forward.

Conclusion

Red Cat has seen its revenues and market grow drastically. It has started to reach scale with its revenues. Those revenues are expected to continue to grow at a rapid pace in 2026, with expectations of over 250% revenue growth. This growth is being driven by the overall market in which Red Cat operates. Epic Furry and the oil crisis really drive home the demand that countries will have for the technology and products that Red Cat offers. War is evolving, and the company has positioned itself well to be a part of that evolution.

The company has large losses as it operates on a thin gross margin and has high operating expenses. The gross margin is something to watch seriously in the second half of the year. As the company begins to manufacture at scale, I would expect this to start to increase. With the thin gross margins and high operating expenses, the company will have to continue strong growth for multiple years before looking at a profit. This is the biggest concern with the company at the moment. It does have a strong cash position to last while the company works towards profitability.

While the valuation is not cheap for the company and it has plenty of risks, I think the long-term market potential outweighs the risks at this point. The market demand is there and will only continue to grow. I previously entered a position when I covered the stock and have seen the stock climb since then. I think it is still worth a position as an investment in the drone market. Likewise, I rate the company a buy.

data.title: Salesforce: AI Is A True Acceleration Driver, Not A Hollow Rebrand
data.description:
While it’s true that the stock market as a whole has shaken off the rising geopolitical tensions in Iran plus a weak macro in rather quick fashion, the notion of the “SaaSpocalypse” is still very much alive, and in the software sector, a deep bear market has escaped very few stocks.

Salesforce (CRM), often considered the pioneer of cloud applications technology and a premier leader in the space, hasn’t been spared. Long a growth stock that traded at heady revenue multiples, Salesforce’s ~30% decline since the start of the year has now positioned the stock at earnings and cash flow multiples that are well below overall S&P 500 averages. The question is, is this a permanent re-rating for Salesforce, and can it thrive in the AI revolution?

I last wrote a "Buy" article on Salesforce in January, when the stock was still trading above $240 per share. Since then, Salesforce has continued a frustrating slide downward, even as founder and CEO Marc Benioff continues to assure the market that it’s wrong on the endgame impacts of AI on software. Amid the sliding valuation on top of clear evidence that Salesforce is not facing wholesale disruption, I’m reiterating my "Buy" opinion on Salesforce.

Zooming Into Salesforce’s Pivot to Agentforce

It’s now impossible to discuss the prospects of any software company without first discussing the “SaaSpocalypse” and what impacts AI will have, if any, on the business.

I’ll back up for a minute to my broader macro view: In my opinion, the SaaSpocalypse is overstated. AI will have an impact on software company business models, but the impact will be more limited and selective - constrained largely to companies that primarily price their products on a per-seat basis.

While Salesforce’s legacy cloud products like CRM and even newer acquired products like Slack are seat-based, Salesforce has two things going for it. The first is that Salesforce is a large enterprise business. The companies that drive Salesforce’s revenue, including those in highly regulated industries like big banks, have Salesforce products deeply ingrained into everyday operations. It’s simply not realistic or even cost-efficient to rip out the software tools that are so integrated into internal and third-party systems to replace them with agentic AI tools.

Note as well that software frequently has issues, and part of the deal with an annual subscription package is ongoing professional support and maintenance. AI has reduced the barriers to entry and cost for building apps, but it doesn’t really move the needle on the economics of build vs. buy. Not many companies will want to choose the DIY route, and this is clearly evidenced in Salesforce’s revenue trends and, in particular, its RPO, which signals healthy backlog growth as a commitment from Salesforce customers (more detail in the next section).

The second, and perhaps more salient point, is that Salesforce was quick to begin pivoting its pricing model. Where I think the “SaaSpocalypse” will have the most impact is not in directly replacing software products but the employees who use them. The past few months have brought deep layoffs at companies like Block (XYZ), Oracle (ORCL), Meta (META), Snap (SNAP), and others. This will hurt the companies that price their products primarily on a per-seat basis and were slow to react to more consumption-based pricing models. Smaller companies that I think are particularly vulnerable include the likes of monday.com (MNDY) and Asana (ASAN).

Again, Salesforce has elements of this legacy pricing in its older products like Sales Cloud (recently rebranded as Agentforce Sales). But it’s quickly adapting. Agentforce is becoming a true driver of accelerating revenue for the company. And unlike Salesforce’s original product stack, it’s priced per usage.

Customers have a choice to pay “per action,” with costs starting at $500 for a package of 100k credits (translating to $0.10 per agentic action) or $2 per complete conversation. Yes, Salesforce will feel pressure from customers laying off headcount, particularly in sales departments, if clients lay off growth functions amid a weak macro. At the same time, these companies are also likely to replace human headcount with agentic automation, giving Salesforce a perfect offset for declining seats.

Evidence for growth in Salesforce/Agentforce usage is directly clear in the company’s data. The company notes that agentic work units (the actions priced at $0.10 each for customers purchasing flex credits) rose 57% quarter over quarter to 771 million actions. We see this growth spurt continuing, and Salesforce gradually migrating its revenue model from seats to credits. We don’t see enough of this proactivity in pricing in other software companies where I have less conviction.

At the end of Q4, Agentforce reached $800 million in ARR. This is still a small slice of the company’s ~$46 billion revenue guide for FY27, but by far the most rapidly growing segment at a stunning 169% y/y growth rate:

Growth Trends and RPO Suggest AI as a Net Tailwind

Next let’s dig into more of the concrete financial evidence that AI and Agentforce provide tailwinds rather than headwinds to Salesforce. Key to this assertion is the fact that in Q4 (the January quarter), Salesforce achieved 11% in constant currency sales growth, accelerating two points sequentially from 9% growth in Q3 (though the company benefited partially from closing its acquisition of Informatica in mid-November). We note critically that Salesforce has infused Agentforce agentic capabilities into legacy products like Sales Cloud, helping to tilt even legacy segments toward consumption-based pricing.

We additionally have high conviction thanks to the company’s recent slew of large deal closings. Per Benioff’s remarks on the Q4 earnings call:

The biggest brands in the world are choosing Salesforce to lead their agentic transformation, companies like Amazon, Ford, AT&T, Moderna, GM, Pfizer, so many, and these are big deals in Q4, wins over $1 million were up 26% year-over-year. That's just so we know in Q4, wins over $1 million were up 26% year-over-year.

Congratulations, Miguel. Wins over $10 million were up 33% year-over-year. For example, the U.S. Army run by Army Secretary, Dan Driscoll, is doing an amazing job, has awarded us a 10-year indefinite delivery, indefinite quantity contract with a ceiling of $5.6 billion.

These large, long-term deals are powering RPO (backlog) growth for Salesforce. RPO in Q4 grew 14% y/y, outpacing subscription revenue growth by 3 points, while the current RPO that will convert to revenue within 12 months grew at an even faster 16% pace.

On top of this, Salesforce is achieving this growth acceleration while still keeping efficiency as a top priority. Pro forma operating margins rose 90bps y/y to 34.1%, driven by economies of scale on sales and marketing costs on an enlarged revenue base.

Valuation and Key Takeaways

At current share prices near $187, Salesforce trades at a market cap of $153.07 billion. Netting off the $9.57 billion of cash and $14.44 billion of debt on the company’s balance sheet yields an enterprise value of $157.94 billion.

For FY27 (the year ending in January 2027), Salesforce has guided to $45.8-$46.2 million in revenue, or 10%-11% y/y growth. It's expecting $13.15 in pro forma EPS ($7.89 GAAP) and ~10% FCF growth, implying $15.77 billion FCF.

Salesforce's valuation multiples sit at:

  • 3.4x EV/FY27 revenue
  • 10.0x EV/FY27 FCF
  • 14.3x FY27 P/E

These valuations sit below most other large-cap tech stocks:

To me, Salesforce's clear advances in Agentforce, and in particular the company's emphasis on switching to usage-based pricing, offer a compelling bulwark against the broad layoffs that I fear are the true impact of AI on the software industry, which primarily impacts companies on legacy seat-based pricing. Salesforce's AI actions and Agentforce are truly driving business acceleration and longevity; it's not just a hollow rebrand or paying lip service to AI, as many software companies are doing at the moment. Stay long here and use decimated valuation multiples as a buying opportunity.

data.title: JEDI: The Next-Generation Aerospace And Defense ETF Positioned For Growth
data.description:
The Defiance Drone and Modern Warfare ETF (JEDI) is a passively managed exchange-traded fund designed to provide exposure to the next generation of warfare technology in unmanned vehicles, defense & communications satellites, and electronic warfare, among other verticals in the market. With growing interest in unmanned aerial vehicles [UAVs] & surface vehicles [USVs] and direct-to-device satellite communications, I believe JEDI can be an appealing investment strategy for investors seeking to diversify their portfolio into the aerospace & defense market. Given the strong tailwinds in the industry, I am recommending JEDI with a Buy rating.

Investment Thesis for JEDI

Defense spending is becoming a more critical topic of discussion in Washington as the war in Iran drags on. With ongoing negotiations taking place and the potential for a long, drawn-out war, as well as the necessity to modernize defense systems for the next-generation battlefield, I believe that companies that participate in munitions manufacturing, UAVs, satcom, and other growth verticals can experience substantial capital growth over the coming years.

At a high level, the defense budget for 2026 was increased to nearly $1T. What’s most appealing is that the budget request for 2027 is expected to grow by 50% to $1.5T with an emphasis on mobilizing domestic manufacturing for defense purposes. The outlook is so grand that the US Department of War is broadening its scope, reaching out to General Motors Company (GM) and Ford Motor Company (F) to mobilize manufacturing for munitions and weapons production. It’s clear that rebuilding defenses in the US is becoming a more critical topic and that investments in next-generation warfare are set for growth.

The security holdings in JEDI are largely tied to advanced weaponry, AI, satellite communications, imaging and defenses, unmanned vehicles, rockets, and munitions. Thematically, this all ties into the goal of developing the Golden Dome program by 2029, which is expected to have an initial $175 billion investment outlay. While I believe 2029 is overly ambitious given the complexity of the system and the risk of failure, the theme remains as a high-growth opportunity for companies that participate in the space economy, as well as munitions, integrated defense systems, and software that will effectively orchestrate the constellation.

For example, JEDI’s top holding, Redwire Corporation (RDW), develops various spacecraft infrastructure, like RF systems & cameras, and in-space propulsion systems that can be utilized throughout the development of the constellation. In addition to this, RDW develops UASs and recently announced a $20 million follow-on order for advanced navigation systems for the Stalker VXE30 UASs from the US Navy and Marine Corps Small Tactical Unmanned Aircraft Systems Program Office. As a systems manufacturer, RDW typically contracts through the Primes to develop components for larger aircraft and spacecraft systems. RDW shares account for 9.56% of the total portfolio weight.

Ondas Inc. (ONDS) is the second largest position in the portfolio with a weight of 8.56%. Ondas develops private networks and autonomous systems purpose-built for defense systems. The company develops both UASs and counter-UASs for commercial and defense use, used for inspection, detection, and surveillance. Consensus estimates suggest substantial growth in the coming years, driven by growing demand for UASs on the battlefield.

Additional large holdings in the drone market include AeroVironment, Inc. (AVAV) at 3.58%, Red Cat Holdings, Inc. (RCAT) at 5.69%, and Kratos Defense & Security Solutions, Inc. (KTOS) at 1.80%.

Other markets that JEDI has exposure to include electric vertical lift through investments in Archer Aviation Inc. (ACHR) at 5.15% and Vertical Aerospace Ltd. (EVTL) at 1.46%, satellite imaging and communications in AST SpaceMobile, Inc. (ASTS) at 3.83% and Planet Labs PBC (PL) at 2.86%, and defense primes like L3Harris Technologies, Inc. (LHX) at 0.56% and RTX Corporation (RTX) at 0.52%.

Overall, I believe that defense spending will facilitate growth in the companies held in JEDI. With defense modernization and munitions manufacturing taking precedence, I believe that JEDI is well-positioned to realize durable growth over the coming years, particularly if the Golden Dome is further developed.

About Defiance Drone and Modern Warfare ETF

JEDI was launched by Defiance ETFs on September 25, 2025, on the NASDAQ Exchange. The strategy has an expense ratio of 69 bps, on par with peer aerospace & defense ETFs on the market.

JEDI exhibits relatively limited liquidity with $104 million in net assets, with an average of $3.28 million in share value changing hands on a daily basis. Given its liquidity, JEDI exhibits a relatively wide bid/ask spread of 0.45%, potentially adding to the total costs of trading the ETF.

JEDI was designed to track the performance of the BITA Drone and Modern Warfare Select Index, a liquidity-momentum-weighted index. This means that constituents are weighted based on their turnover ratio, or the stock’s liquidity, relative to their market capitalization. The index caps weightings at 10% of the total portfolio weight and limits the aggregate weight of constituents over 5% to 45% of the total index weight. The index is rebalanced semi-annually.

The index covers a wide variety of subsectors within the aerospace & defense industry, including:

  • Drones & Unmanned Aerial Systems.
  • AI-Driven Warfare & Military IT.
  • Unmanned Surface, Ground, & Underwater Systems.
  • Electronic & Communication Warfare Solutions.
  • Intelligence, Surveillance, and Reconnaissance.
  • Space & Defense Infrastructure.
  • Military Cybersecurity.
  • Military Robotics.
  • Electric & Urban Air Mobility.

Risks Related to JEDI

JEDI is a thematic ETF designed to provide exposure to a specific market within the aerospace & defense industry, presenting certain risks that should be considered before making a final investment decision. Companies held in the ETF are largely tied to government contracts and may experience significant volatility from quarter to quarter based on the timing of the contracts. Companies may also be exposed to risks related to government shutdowns, which may push out contract bidding and awards. The portfolio is considered to be concentrated in a single sector and may exhibit volatility in unison. JEDI has relatively thin liquidity and may expose investors to additional trading costs when actively managing a position.

Final Thoughts

JEDI can be an appealing investment strategy for those seeking to gain exposure to the long-term, secular trend of increased defense spending, defense modernization, munitions manufacturing, and drone adoption. While JEDI has relatively thin liquidity, I believe the strategy can pay off as a buy-and-hold investment, as growth is expected to be a multi-year trend. I am recommending JEDI with a Buy rating.

data.title: From Oil Shock To Record Highs: Markets Respond To Conflict
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from The Boyar Value Group's Q1 2026 Letter.

War, Oil, and the Strait of Hormuz

The most dramatic development of the quarter came on February 28th, when President Trump launched Operation Epic Fury, a joint military offensive with Israel against Iran. The strikes were extensive, targeting both military infrastructure and senior Iranian leadership. For purposes of this letter, we are focused on the economic consequences of the conflict, though the humanitarian toll is obviously significant.

Those economic consequences were immediate, particularly in the energy markets. In response to the U.S. attack, Iran moved to assert control over the Strait of Hormuz, the narrow waterway through which a meaningful portion of the world’s oil supply passes. The resulting disruption in tanker traffic sent energy prices sharply higher.

Unsurprisingly, energy stocks were the quarter’s standout performers, gaining 37.2%. Materials, Utilities, and Consumer Staples also held up relatively well. On the other side of the ledger, Financials, Consumer Discretionary, and Technology were among the weakest areas of the market.

The geopolitical picture has shifted somewhat since quarter-end. A ceasefire has been announced, and markets have responded positively, with the S&P 500 reaching a new record, even as many individual companies remain dramatically below their all-time highs. At the same time, after failed peace talks held in Pakistan, the U.S. has blockaded Iranian ports, while traffic through the Strait remains severely disrupted, underscoring just how fragile the situation remains. Whether this ceasefire holds, and for how long, is impossible to know. We would caution against reading too much into the market’s short-term reaction in either direction.
